title: The difference between a trigger and an emotional flashback
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
The first time I heard the word "trigger" I wanted to throw something

Because it felt like everyone was using it wrong. My therapist would say "you were triggered" and I'd nod, but inside I was screaming. No. That wasn't what happened. Something else happened. Something older and deeper and way more terrifying than just feeling upset.

I remember sitting in my car after a grocery store trip, shaking so hard I couldn't get the key in the ignition. A man had walked past me in the cereal aisle. He wore the same cologne as my uncle. That was it. Just a smell. And suddenly I was 8 years old again, small and helpless, my body flooding with chemicals that didn't belong in a grocery store on a Tuesday afternoon.

Here's the thing. That wasn't a trigger. Well, technically the smell was a trigger. But what happened inside me was an emotional flashback. And confusing the two kept me stuck for years.

Let me break this down in a way I wish someone had explained to me a decade ago.

What a trigger actually is

A trigger is a stimulus. That's it. It's a sensory cue, a situation, a tone of voice, a date on the calendar, a song on the radio. Something external that your nervous system has learned to associate with danger. Your brain says "oh, that thing again. We know what that means." And it fires off an alarm.

Peter Levine, who literally wrote the book on somatic experiencing, talks about triggers as incomplete biological responses. Your body started a survival reaction at some point in the past, and it never got to finish. So every time something reminds your nervous system of that moment, it tries again. It's like a computer program that keeps crashing and restarting at the same error.

Triggers can be obvious. A raised voice. The smell of alcohol. Someone standing too close. A door slamming. But they can also be incredibly subtle. A certain quality of light in the afternoon. The way someone clears their throat. The feeling of a particular fabric against your skin. Your nervous system doesn't care about logic. It cares about pattern matching.

I once had a client who couldn't figure out why she dissociated every time she walked into a specific coffee shop. We traced it back to the color of the walls. It was the same shade of yellow as her childhood bedroom. The room where things happened that she couldn't talk about for another twenty years. Wild, right?

So a trigger is the key. It's the thing that unlocks the door. But the door itself, and what's behind it, that's the emotional flashback.

Emotional flashbacks are a whole different animal

Pete Walker, who wrote extensively about complex PTSD, coined the term "emotional flashback" to describe something that happens to trauma survivors that doesn't look like a typical PTSD flashback. In a standard flashback, you might see images, hear sounds, feel physical sensations from the original event. But emotional flashbacks don't come with visual replay. They come with feelings. Intense, overwhelming, seemingly out of proportion feelings that don't match what's happening in the present moment.

You're not seeing the past. You're feeling it. Your body is reliving the emotional state you were in during the trauma, but your conscious mind has no idea why.

This is the part that messes with people the most. You get triggered, and suddenly you feel worthless. Or terrified. Or enraged. Or numb. And you look around at your life, at the person who just asked you a simple question, at the meeting you're sitting in, and you think "why am I reacting like this? What is wrong with me?"

Nothing is wrong with you. You're having an emotional flashback.

The trigger was the key. The emotional flashback is the prison you find yourself inside. And the worst part is, you often don't even realize you're in prison. You think the feelings are about right now. You think your partner actually is dangerous. You think you actually are a failure. You think the world actually is ending.

It's not. It's the past leaking into the present through a crack you didn't know existed.

I've had emotional flashbacks that lasted hours. Days. One time, after a relatively minor conflict with a friend, I spent three weeks convinced that everyone I loved was going to abandon me. Three weeks. And I knew, intellectually, that my friend and I had resolved the issue. But my nervous system didn't care about intellectual. My nervous system was running a script from 1994.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through exactly how the body stores these unfinished experiences and why talk therapy alone often isn't enough to resolve them. It's not light reading, but it's the kind of book that makes you feel less crazy.

How to tell the difference in real time

This is where the rubber meets the road. Because knowing the difference intellectually is one thing. Feeling it in your body while you're in the middle of a reaction is another thing entirely.

Here's a quick test I use with myself and with the people I work with. Ask yourself three questions.

First, does this feeling have a size? Not an intensity, but a size. Emotional flashbacks feel enormous. They feel like they're filling up the whole room. They feel like they're going to swallow you whole. A triggered response, even a strong one, usually feels more contained. It feels like a reaction, not a possession.

Second, does this feeling have an age? Emotional flashbacks feel young. They feel like a child's feelings in an adult's body. If you stop and check in, you might notice that the fear you're feeling isn't your adult fear. It's a 6-year-old's fear. It's a teenager's shame. It's a baby's terror of being left alone. Sit with that for a second. Notice what just happened in your body.

Third, does this feeling have a story? Triggers often have a clear narrative. "He raised his voice, and I got scared because my dad used to yell." That's a trigger. That's straightforward. An emotional flashback doesn't come with a story. It comes with a feeling state that seems to have no origin. You're just suddenly drowning in shame or rage or despair, and you can't connect it to anything that's happening right now.

If you answered yes to all three, you're probably in an emotional flashback. And the most important thing you can do is stop trying to figure out why you feel this way and start trying to ground yourself in the present moment.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, talks about the importance of ventral vagal cues. These are signals that tell your nervous system "you are safe right now." A soft voice. A slow exhale. A gentle touch. The feeling of your feet on the floor. Your brain needs evidence that the present moment is not the past.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is this breathing exercise tool (paid link). It creates resistance on the exhale, which activates the vagus nerve and helps shift your body out of fight-or-flight. I keep one in my bag for rough days.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that promotes calm without sedation. I take it when I feel that edge of hyperarousal creeping in, that feeling that tells me a flashback might be on its way.

Why the wellness industry gets this so wrong

I need to be direct about something. The way "triggered" gets thrown around in pop psychology makes me want to scream. Someone says something mildly uncomfortable and they say they're "triggered." A memory makes them sad and they call it a "trigger." And I get it, language evolves, but this blurring of terms has real consequences for people who actually have trauma.

When everything is a trigger, nothing is a trigger. And people who are having genuine emotional flashbacks, people who are reliving their worst moments in their bodies without any visual cue, they start to think they're broken. They think "if everyone else is handling their triggers fine, why am I falling apart?"

Because you're not just triggered. You're flashing back. And that's a different animal entirely.

The wellness industry wants you to think you can breathe your way out of a flashback. And sure, breathing helps. It's a tool. But if you're in the middle of an emotional flashback, you might not even be able to find your breath. Your body might be so far into a survival response that conscious breathing feels impossible. That's not a failure. That's biology.

Janina Fisher, who specializes in trauma-informed treatment, talks about the importance of first recognizing that you're in a flashback before you try to do anything about it. The recognition itself is the intervention. Just saying to yourself "oh, I'm having an emotional flashback" can create enough distance to start coming back to the present.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help ground you when you're spinning out. I use mine when I feel that floaty, disconnected feeling that sometimes precedes a flashback.

Look, I'm not saying triggers aren't real. They are. They're incredibly real. But if you've been calling everything a trigger and wondering why you're still struggling, I want you to consider the possibility that what you're experiencing is something more. Something that needs a different kind of care.

Emotional flashbacks require grounding. They require orienting to the present. They require reminding your body, over and over again, that the danger is over. That you survived. That you're here, now, in a body that is older and stronger and more capable than the one that went through the original trauma.

And they require patience. So much patience. Because emotional flashbacks don't go away just because you understand them. They fade. They get shorter. They lose their grip. But they don't disappear overnight. Recovery is not a straight line. It's a spiral. You'll circle back to the same material again and again, but each time from a slightly higher vantage point.

I still have emotional flashbacks. Not as often. Not as long. Not as intense. But they still come. And when they do, I don't fight them anymore. I don't try to figure out what triggered them. I don't spiral into shame about having them. I just say "oh, hello. I know you. You're the feeling of being 8 years old in a body that's 38. You're not the truth. You're just an echo."

And then I put my feet on the floor. I take a slow breath. I touch something soft. I look around the room and name three things I can see. And I wait. Because the flashback will pass. They always do.

The trigger was just the doorbell. The emotional flashback was the visitor. And you don't have to let that visitor run your house forever.
